description:
Give the people what they want: make sure your social media followers never miss a stream. This Applet will automatically post this message on Twitter and your official Facebook Page when you start a stream:
"I'm now streaming on Twitch! Playing [Game name]" + a link to your Twitch channel
name: Automatically post on Twitter and your Facebook Page when you start a Twitch stream
speed: Polling Applets usually run within 1 hour
rule_description: This automation posts a message on Twitter and your Facebook Page every time you start a Twitch stream. When you begin streaming, a trigger activates that takes note of the game you’re playing and your Twitch channel details. As a result, it automatically sends out a tweet saying, "I'm now streaming on Twitch! Playing [Game name]" along with a link to your channel, and simultaneously creates a link post on your Facebook Page with a similar message and link.
user_intent_example: Please post on Twitter and my Facebook Page every time I start a stream on Twitch, ideally with the game's name and a direct link to my channel. I want my followers to see it live!
instruct_zs:
You are an expert JavaScript developer with specialized knowledge of filter code for trigger-action rules.
A trigger-action rule specifies an automation in which a defined condition (the trigger) causes one or more actions to be executed.
 Filter code is used to refine these rules by specifying the conditions under which different actions should be performed or skipped.

Your task is to produce JavaScript filter code that modifies the rule so that it conforms to the following user intent:
Please post on Twitter and my Facebook Page every time I start a stream on Twitch, ideally with the game's name and a direct link to my channel. I want my followers to see it live!

Given the following natural language description of a trigger-action rule:
This automation posts a message on Twitter and your Facebook Page every time you start a Twitch stream. When you begin streaming, a trigger activates that takes note of the game you’re playing and your Twitch channel details. As a result, it automatically sends out a tweet saying, "I'm now streaming on Twitch! Playing [Game name]" along with a link to your channel, and simultaneously creates a link post on your Facebook Page with a similar message and link.


You must rely exclusively on the following accessors to define the trigger conditions:
- JS accessor: `Twitch.newStreamByYou.CreatedAt` | type: Date with time (ISO8601) | description: Date and time event was created.
- JS accessor: `Twitch.newStreamByYou.Game` | type: String | description: The name of the game being streamed.
- JS accessor: `Twitch.newStreamByYou.ChannelName` | type: String | description: The name of the channel broadcasting the stream.
- JS accessor: `Twitch.newStreamByYou.StreamPreview` | type: Image URL | description: The URL to an image of a preview of the stream.
- JS accessor: `Twitch.newStreamByYou.ChannelUrl` | type: Web URL | description: The URL to the channel on Twitch.
- JS accessor: `Twitch.newStreamByYou.CurrentViewers` | type: String | description: The current number of viewers of the stream.

The action behavior must use only the following methods:
- JS method: `Twitter.postNewTweet.setTweet(string: tweet)` | helper: Tweet text
- JS method: `FacebookPages.createLinkPage.setLinkUrl(string: link_url)` | helper: Link URL
- JS method: `FacebookPages.createLinkPage.setMessage(string: message)` | helper: Message

If the user intent requires skipping actions, you may use:
-`Twitter.postNewTweet.skip`
-`FacebookPages.createLinkPage.skip`

Usage constraints:
- For any time-based condition, you MUST use `Meta.currentUserTime` to access the current date and time.
  Do NOT use `Date`, `new Date()`, or any other time source.
- Treat `Meta.currentUserTime` as a Moment.js instance and use only its helper methods
  (e.g. `hour()`, `minute()`, `week()`, `isoWeekday()`, `dayOfYear()`, `format(...)`).


Write ONLY the JavaScript Filter Code that implements the required constraints.
Do not include any explanation, natural language, comments, JSON or other non-code elements.

description: Inspects all new events on your calendar to make sure they include an agenda. If an agenda appears to be missing a Slack alert is sent with details.
name: Get Slack notifications for new calendar events without an agenda
speed: Polling Applets usually run within 1 hour
rule_description: This automation monitors your Google Calendar for any new events added. Whenever a new event is created, it checks if the event has an agenda in its description. If the agenda is missing, it triggers a Slack alert that posts details about the event, including its title, description, and time, to a designated Slack channel.
user_intent_example: I'd like to receive a notification in Slack whenever I add a new calendar event that doesn't have a detailed agenda. Can you set this up so that the alert includes the event title and when it starts?

description: When MyQ garage door closes, switch off driveway Philips Hue lights and log garage door closing event to an iOS calendar. The event duration in the calendar log is set for 5 minutes to make it readable.
name: Switch off driveway Hue lights when MyQ garage door closes and log event to iOS calendar
speed: Realtime Applets usually run within 10 seconds
rule_description: When the MyQ garage door is closed, the automation will turn off the Philips Hue lights in the driveway. Additionally, it will log this closing event in the iOS calendar, creating a new event titled 'Garage Door Closed' that lasts for 5 minutes. The event will include relevant details such as the time of closure and the specific door that was closed.
user_intent_example: Whenever I close my garage door, I want the driveway lights to turn off automatically, and I’d like it noted in my iOS calendar for 5 minutes, so I have a record of when it happened.

speed: Realtime Applets usually run within 10 seconds
rule_description: This automation toggles your lights on or off whenever you press the designated button on your device. The action is executed every time the button is pressed, regardless of the current state of the lights. The trigger captures details such as the location (latitude and longitude) where the button was pressed, the time of the press, and provides a shareable link to the button's page and a map to the location.
user_intent_example: Whenever I'm at home or in my garden, I want to simply press the button on my phone to quickly turn the living room lights on or off, depending on their current state. Please set this up so it works anytime I'm using the IFTTT app.

 Filter code is used to refine these rules by specifying the conditions under which different actions should be performed or skipped.

description: Create tasks in bulk for Asana from CSV files and Google Sheets.

rule_description: This automation creates a new task in Asana for every row that is imported from a CSV file or Google Sheet. The trigger activates whenever a row is added, and it utilizes various details from the row, such as 'Field1', 'Field2', and others, to populate the task's name and notes. Additionally, the due date can be set based on the row's data, and users have the option to attach a file to the task if necessary.

Each row should become a separate task, and if possible, I'd like to have the first field in the row as the task name and the second field as notes. Also, if there's a due date in the last column, please set that for the task.

description: Turn on heat at your thermostat when it is too cold at the room where a Wireless Tag is located. You must enable temperature monitoring and set the lower threshold temperature at the Tag from inside the Wireless Tag app. This Applet will set the temperature at the thermostat 10 degree F higher than the current temperature at the Tag to ensure heat will be turned on (the temperature of the room where thermostat is located may have up to 9 degree higher temperature).
name: When tag is too cold, turn on heat
speed: Realtime Applets usually run within 10 seconds
rule_description: This automation activates your Honeywell thermostat to heat the room when the ambient temperature detected by a Wireless Tag falls below a specified lower threshold. When this trigger occurs, the thermostat will be set to a temperature that is 10 degrees Fahrenheit higher than the current temperature at the Wireless Tag, ensuring that the heating system engages even if the thermostat is located in a warmer area. To use this feature, temperature monitoring must be enabled within the Wireless Tag app, and users must set the appropriate threshold temperature.
user_intent_example: Please turn on the heat whenever the Wireless Tag in my living room detects a temperature below 18 degrees Celsius, and keep it at least 10 degrees higher than the current reading for a couple of hours.

description: Door Counter
name: Door Counter
speed: Realtime Applets usually run within 10 seconds
rule_description: This automation counts door clicks by updating a specified cell in a spreadsheet every time the designated Flic button is clicked. The trigger activates whenever the button is pressed, capturing the location (longitude and latitude), the date and time of the click, and the type of click (single, double, or hold). Consequently, it updates a cell in the first worksheet of a specified spreadsheet with the current count or relevant data each time the button is pressed, creating a new spreadsheet if it does not already exist.

description: Every JotForm submission creates a new contact or updates a contact in MailChimp
name: Create Mailchimp contacts with JotForm
speed: Polling Applets usually run within 1 hour
rule_description: This automation triggers whenever there is a new submission to a JotForm. Upon receiving a new submission, it either creates a new contact or updates an existing contact in MailChimp. The trigger captures various details about the submission, including the respondent's IP address, the form title, the submission date and time, and all submitted data in different formats. The resulting action adds or updates a subscriber in a specified MailChimp list using the email address, first name, and last name provided in the submission.

description: Set the night time mode
name: Night Time
speed: Realtime Applets usually run within 10 seconds
rule_description: This automation sets the night mode for your home lighting whenever the designated Flic button is clicked. If the Flic is clicked at any time (with no restrictions on the click type - whether it be a single click, double click, or hold), it will trigger the lights. The specific action taken depends on whether the lights are currently on or off: a single click will turn on the lights if they are off, while a subsequent click will toggle them off if they are on. The automation allows you to control your Hue lights through simple clicks of the Flic button.
user_intent_example: I want my Flic button to turn on the lights when I click it at night, so I can have the right light setting for relaxing after dinner.

 Filter code is used to refine these rules by specifying the conditions under which different actions should be performed or skipped.

Your task is to produce JavaScript filter code that modifies the rule so that it conforms to the following user intent:
Please turn off my living room MagicHue light whenever Apilio notifies that the 'Evening Transition' event occurs, especially between 6 PM and 8 PM.

Given the following natural language description of a trigger-action rule:
This automation turns off a selected MagicHue light whenever a specific event is received from Apilio. The trigger is activated upon receiving an event from Apilio, which includes details such as the event name and three associated values. Once this event is detected, the action executed is to switch off the designated MagicHue light.


You must rely exclusively on the following accessors to define the trigger conditions:
- JS accessor: `Apilio.iftttActionReports.CreatedAt` | type: Date with time (ISO8601) | description: Date and time event was created.
- JS accessor: `Apilio.iftttActionReports.EventName` | type: String | description: Name of the event that was received.
- JS accessor: `Apilio.iftttActionReports.ApilioValue1` | type: String | description: Value 1 received from Apilio
- JS accessor: `Apilio.iftttActionReports.ApilioValue2` | type: String | description: Value 2 received from Apilio
- JS accessor: `Apilio.iftttActionReports.ApilioValue3` | type: String | description: Value 3 received from Apilio


If the user intent requires skipping actions, you may use:
-`Magichue.poweroff.skip`

Usage constraints:
- For any time-based condition, you MUST use `Meta.currentUserTime` to access the current date and time.
  Do NOT use `Date`, `new Date()`, or any other time source.
- Treat `Meta.currentUserTime` as a Moment.js instance and use only its helper methods
  (e.g. `hour()`, `minute()`, `week()`, `isoWeekday()`, `dayOfYear()`, `format(...)`).

description: Mute Sonos when Swann Security camera detects sound
name: Mute Sonos when Swann Security camera detects sound
speed: Polling Applets usually run within 1 hour
rule_description: This automation mutes the Sonos speaker system whenever the Swann security camera detects sound. It activates when the camera senses a noise, and in response, the Sonos player or group is muted to ensure a quieter environment.

description: Automatically open garage door when Bouncie-enabled vehicle enter geo-circle
name: Open Garage On Bouncie Return
speed: Realtime Applets usually run within 10 seconds
rule_description: This automation will open your garage door automatically whenever a Bouncie-enabled vehicle enters a designated geo-circle. The trigger is activated when the vehicle crosses the boundary of the geo-circle, and as a result, the garage door will open, providing convenient access as you arrive home.

description: Turn on a LIFX light when a specific person comes home and unlocks a designated August Smart Lock.
name: Turn on your lights with August Smart Lock
speed: Polling Applets usually run within 1 hour
rule_description: This automation will activate a LIFX light whenever a specific person unlocks the designated August Smart Lock at your home. The trigger is initiated when the lock is unlocked by that person, capturing the date and time of the event, along with the user's identity and the associated lock and house names.
